This is an introduction to Mo SMB to get you started with the Mo SMB server on any server platform like Cent OS, Ubuntu, and Debian. Mo SMB is a proprietary ANSI C SMB v3 Server implementation in user space only and supported on major Linux flavors. It is designed to be fully compliant with Microsoft Server Message Block (SMB) version 3 Protocol specifications.

It is designed to be a high performance, scalable, highly available, user-space server implementation that has an API for third-party integration. The entire Mo SMB configuration can be categorized into seven major categories, namely:

Support for VFS filesystems (Ext4, XFS, ZFS, Gluster FS, Lustre FS, and so on) RDMA data transformation using the above mentioned signing and encryption ciphers SMB open file modes, SMB locks supported across cluster nodes Highly Salable as per system resources available (no of connections, no of sessions, no of simultaneous file opens etc.) Distributed Mo SMB file shares as Distributed File System Mo SMB supports S3 Object Store interface (Add On: S3 plugin) S3 driver plug-in for Object Stores like AWS, Minio, and so on Config CLI support for Mo SMB configuration (mosmbcli) Mo SMB metrices Integration support available for monitoring tools like Checkmk, Prometheus SMB Tree Connect Redirection (Load Balancing using storage node affinity)

MS SQL 2012 and above databases hosted on the Mo SMB SMB3 shares. Hyper-V (managed by SCVMM). VM stored on the Mo SMB SMB3 shares. Use cases such as VM Cloning, Template, Live Migration, and so on. High performance File Server for User Home Directory and Media Streaming. Continuously Available (CA) for Client Applications using the Witness Server.
and Network Time Protocol Service (NTP), LDAP, SSSD

The following is a comprehensive list of port numbers used by the Mo SMB server and Mo SMB related miscellaneous services: From version 091 onwards, Mo SMB will work with LDAPS. Mo SMB will attempt Secure LDAP (LDAP over SSL/TLS) communication to the AD server (to port 636) and if it is not setup then will fallback to non secure LDAP communication over port 389. The port 636 communication setup to AD can be checked with command openssl s_client -connect <AD_IP>:636

Mo SMB does NOT recommend and support concurrent multiprotocol write access of the same file or directory via SMB and NFS/native posix client/backend access. This is because mosmb supports distributed SMB locking, byte-range locks, file sharing modes etc. and other protocols do not. Concurrent access over other protocols (or natively) and modifying files/folders without unified locking can cause data inconsistency for Windows applications that are accessing over SMB (mosmb).

Background and Recommendation In an enterprise environment, it is common that Linux, Windows and mac OS workloads require access to the same data. It is recommended that multiprotocol access NOT be used for this purpose. Instead, either Mo SMB or NFS, but not both should be used to access a particular file. This is because Mo SMB and NFS use different locking mechanisms and using both to access a file concurrently can lead to data inconsistency issues.

That is why, for example, Amazon EFS, Amazon FSx, or Azure Files, provide access to files via either NFS or SMB, not both. Since it is not possible to control access at the granularity of a single file or directory, it is recommended that data should be partitioned at the volume level so that each volume is accessed via NFS or Mo SMB, but not both.

Mo SMB supports access to all 3- Linux, Windows and mac OS clients in a consistent manner, so there should be no data consistency issue when these different OS clients access the same file or directory concurrently. The above data consistency issue can also occur when accessing files concurrently via Mo SMB and natively using the POSIX client. It is recommended that for such access also only one of the access mechanisms be used for a particular volume and not both.

1 RDMA (smb Direct) 1.1 Enable SMB Direct in mosmb.json 1.2 SMB Direct ring size 1.3 SMB Direct buffer size 1.4 TCP parameters

Note : Refer to the Troubleshooting Guide for the RDMA (SMBDirect) Communication on a Mo SMB Server to understand how to make the RDMA (SMBDirect) basic setup work. The SMBDirect protocol uses the RDMA Read/Write requests to transfer file data within the client and the server. Every RDMA Read/Write operation consists of a SMB2 Read request with 1 or more buffer descriptors . Each buffer descriptor contains information for an I/O buffer with the desired transfer size.

Each buffer descriptor thus requires to be allocated and pinned in memory till the transfer is completed through the RDMA channel. So for every buffer descriptor a buffer of desired size is allocated and pinned, this is a control operation and consumes a significant amount of time. Therefore we have introduced the following parameters to ensure that we reduce control operations during data operations:
Edit the /etc/mosmb/mosmb.json file , and set enable Smb Direct parameter value to yes

The server should be restarted to reflect the effect when a parameter is changed. The SMB Direct ring size is a parameter that allocates and reserves the specified number of buffers per RDMA connection, each of the SMBDirect buffer size size. On every RDMA Read/Write request the buffer is consumed from this ring and replenished after use. This allows faster operations as we can avoid memory pinning operations (control) in the data path resulting in faster speeds.

This can to be tuned w.r.t the I/O pattern for typical application. Setting it to very higher number can guarantee faster transfers at the cost of higher memory footprint. Whereas setting it to very lower number can lead to slower speeds due to the lack of available buffers for transfers. We recommend it to be set 1024 for media applications, and 256 for non-media operations (basic file server, disk io apps)

Accepts value from 1 to 8 inclusive. This value is in Mega Bytes (for example 1 represents 1MB). The server should be restarted to reflect the effect of a changed parameter. This parameter represents the size of transfer for every buffer descriptor from SMB Direct ring . As per the SMBD Specification this parameter should be less than or equal to the maximum fragmented receive size (1MB). However, this parameter could be less than or equal to the maximum transaction size (8MB) as well.

For RDMA connections only, allocate minimal resources to TCP parameters since the TCP buffers will consume some amount of RAM For example, pre Processor Threads = 4, ring Buffer Count = 256 (or 128 or 64)

All Mo SMB-CL builds supports single domain clustering. To form a single domain Mo SMB cluster simply follow the configurations mentioned in steps below one by one. 1. Carry out Network Time Protocol (NTP) Configuration settings on all Mo SMB server nodes. 2. Request the Domain Administration team for AD and DNS Configurations. Carry out name resolution settings on all Mo SMB servers. 3. ntlm Local and related services configurations on all Mo SMB server machines in the cluster.

For each node in a cluster, you need to create Mo SMB users with the same Username and password All Users should belong to same primary group on all nodes for ACL's to get applied appropriately. All groups and users should should have same uid and gid on all nodes 4. Mo SMB server is Zoo Keeper client; Perform Mo SMB -Zoo Keeper settings 6. Setup the Mo SMB configuration file (/etc/mosmb/mosmb.json) for cluster use.

In global Section set enable High Availability to yes In persistent Store Section keep pstore Path location on the common storage path on all Mo SMB nodes in the cluster. In section node Info Section , assign a different node Id to each node in Mo SMB cluster. In section auth Section , set auth Mode as ntlm Local In share Section keep share Path location on the common storage path on all Mo SMB nodes in the cluster. In share Section set parameters is Continously Available and is Cluster to yes

"globalSection": { .... "enableHighAvailability" : "yes" .... "persistentStoreSection": { "pstorePath": "/mnt/mycluster/MoSMB_mhk", .... "nodeInfoSection": { "nodeId": 1 } "authSection": { "authMode": "ntlmLocal" } "sharesSection": [ { "shareName": "Cluster Share", "comment": "File Share Directory", "sharePath": "/mnt/mycluster/MoSMB_share", "isContinouslyAvailable": "yes", "isCluster": "yes", ....
7. Set Priority List for AD IPs (in an AD Farm) if required If you have a AD farm, and want mosmb to use only few of them for any reason:
Add Priority List for AD IPs in /etc/mosmb

8. Start Mo SMB service on all nodes in the Mo SMB cluster and check service mosmb status and check the mosmb.log for details. You can check details of Mo SMB server Maintenance here

1. Make sure ZooKeeper standalone server / ZooKeeper cluster is running. 2. Make sure pstorePath and sharePath are not the same directory but are on the common storage path. 3. Make sure all the MoSMB configuration on all cluster nodes is similar. 4. To know other mosmb.json parameters in details refer this .

When there are restricted Kerberos entries on the server machine seen as output of command klist -k /etc/krb5.keytab Check if they are also present on the AD side if present we need to remove them from AD. Removing these entries from AD side only is sufficient.

Login to AD machine with user who has admin rights and open Active directory administrative Center In computers open properties of the Mo SMB server machine In Properties of server machine   Extensions   Attribute Editor In Attribute editor select the attribute "service Principal Name" Remove restricted entries from the service Principal Name against the Mo SMB server.

Please note that the performance tuning recommendations provided in this guide are general guidelines. Optimal configuration for your environment may differ depending on your workload, hardware, and other factors. To achieve the best results, it's recommended that you conduct performance tests on your specific configuration and make adjustments as necessary. To tune for best performance at a node level refer Performance tuning for Standalone Mo SMB Server

Mo SMB cluster performance tuning is mainly dependent on the following : Pstore path   A dedicated shared volume is recommended for pstore path. This must be configured for low latency. Mo SMB Zoo Keeper's data Dir (data directory) path   A dedicated device is recommended for the Mo SMB Zoo Keeper data Dir path on each node in the Zookeeper cluster. This could be a low latency device.

In /etc/mosmb/mosmb.json of each node in Mo SMB cluster for Zoo Keeper's configurations one should mention the host Ports in a way so that then Zoo Keeper node closest to Mo SMB should be mentioned first. Mo SMB Zoo Keeper config parameters   Mo SMB provides a mosmb_zoo.cfg file at location /etc/mosmb/ on Mo SMB installation parameters within this file are for reference and must not be modified. Only recommended parameters must be configured mentioned in documentation.

Mo SMB offers you the following features and benefits: Mo SMB is built as an SMB2/SMB3 user mode server stack designed to run crucial enterprise workloads such as Enterprise File Server, Microsoft Hyper-V and SQL Server. Mo SMB is built to be feature-rich, fully compliant, light-weight, low footprint and proprietary license bearing. Mo SMB supports distributed file systems like Lustre FS, Ceph FS, Map R FS and Gluster FS.

Mo SMB can be deployed in the Active-Passive or Active-Active Scale-Out Cluster mode over these file-system clients to provide High Availability (HA) and Transparent Failover of connections enabling users to support their Service-Level Agreements (SLAs).

SSSD can use the SID of an AD user to algorithmically generate POSIX IDs in a process called ID mapping. ID mapping creates a map between SIDs in AD and IDs on Linux. Because the IDs for an AD user are generated in a consistent way from the same SID, the user has the same UID and GID when logging in to any Linux system.

The ID-mapping feature allows SSSD to act as a client of Active Directory without requiring administrators to extend user attributes to support POSIX attributes for user and group identifiers. Make sure the sssd.conf has
NOTE : When ID-mapping is enabled, the uid Number and gid Number attributes are ignored. And sssd synthesizes the uid gid for the user.

For domain user mouser , we can assign ownership to share for access # id mouser@mosmbad.dom uid=1795001104(mouser@mosmbad.dom) gid=1795000513(domain users@mosmbad.dom) #set owndereship to the share path for the AD user, mouser@mosmbad.dom chown mouser@mosmbad.dom:"Domain users@mosmbad.dom" /opt/share #Login to windows client using domain user mouser and access the share

Administrator will map UIDs and GIDs of the linux user to the AD user. AD can create and store POSIX attributes, such as uid Number, gid Number, unix Home Directory, or login Shell. When using this scheme, to keep the AD-defined values, you must disable ID mapping in SSSD.

cat /etc/sssd/sssd.conf [sssd] domains = mosmbad.dom config_file_version = 2 services = nss, pam [domain/mosmbad.dom] ad_domain = mosmbad.dom krb5_realm = MOSMBAD.DOM realmd_tags = manages-system joined-with-samba cache_credentials = True id_provider = ad krb5_store_password_if_offline = True default_shell = /bin/bash ldap_id_mapping = False use_fully_qualified_names = True fallback_homedir = /home/%u@%d access_provider = ad

id test_user1@mosmbad.dom uid=191401645(test_user1@mosmbad.dom) gid=191400513(domain users@mosmbad.dom) groups=191400513(domain users@mosmbad.dom)

If you are using POSIX ACL, and Auth Mode is NTLMLocal : In this case the linux user can be mapped to windows user, by having the same username. Add the User as SMB user using mosmb_passwd. The smb password for the user will be picked up for granting access. So if smb password matches the windows password of the user, then the user will get access to the share seamlessly.

e.g. If user mouser exists in Windows machine (either Local user or AD user), and same mouser is added to linux. useradd mouser passwd mouser mosmb_passwd -a mouser (set SMB password for the user same as windows user password) Change owndership of share path to mouser chown mouser:mouser <path to share> Login to windows machine with mouser , and access the share. This will grant mouser access to the share seamlessly.

Setup and configure Mo SMB server (Standalone mode) in the Kerberos mode for any server Operating System, following steps need to be performed: Note : Kindly refer to Network Configuration to perform the steps underneath. Use your Active Directory (AD) Administrator user account to integrate the Mo SMB server with Active Directory. Note : The Active Directory user should be created in the very same domain in which the Mo SMB Server Computer Account will be created.

Join the Mo SMB server to the Active Directory domain. Remove restricted kerberos keys, if any, from the Active Directory Manager from the domain joined machine. Configure the Mo SMB server as a DNS Client. Add a Mo SMB server DNS Client entry to the DNS server if not auto-registered. Configure NTP so that the machine with Mo SMB is in time sync with the Windows Active Directory server. Note : The maximum time difference allowed is 5 minutes as per Kerberos documentation.

Configure the Kerberos settings by obtaining the Kerberos ticket from the Ticket Granting server (AD-KDC Server). Ensure that you have mentioned the correct storage path in /etc/mosmb/mosmb.json in Share Configuration . Start / Restart the Mo SMB server for the desired changes to take effect.

Note : In case the user mapping of the Windows AD domain user to the Linux user is required in certain IT infrastructures with a mix of Windows and Linux machines, the  sssd  service and its sssd.conf file can be configured appropriately. Mo SMB can take advantage of this user mapping configuration to provide user access control.
Mo SMB supports Microsoft Active Directory (AD) based Kerberos authentication. The Mo SMB server is added to the AD Domain to authenticate and authorize the SMB Client's access to Mo SMB shares.

You must have a working models of the AD in your Evaluation and/or Production environment before you start using the appliance with kerberos authentication. You require Active Directory Domain Administrator user to make related modifications. Use adcli to join the Mo SMB server to multiple AD Domains.

Install the realm and dependent packages on the server machine. Command : yum install adcli OR apt-get install adcli To join the domain with adcli: Command : adcli join --service-name=host -D <AD_domain_name> -U <AD_domain_administrator_user> --host-fqdn= <server_hostname>.<AD_domain_name> Example : adcli join --service-name=host -D MOSMBAD.DOM -U user1@MOSMBAD.DOM --host-fqdn=mymosmbserver.mosmbad.dom Password for user1@MOSMBAD.DOM: Note: The domain join creates a machine entry in the AD.

You must update the DNS entries for forward lookup zone and reverse lookup zone manually. To leave a domain with adcli: Command : adcli delete-computer --domain= <AD_domain_name> -U <AD_domain_administrator_user> Note: This deletes the entry from AD. You must update the DNS entries for forward lookup zone and reverse lookup zone manually.

The Mo SMB services and metrics can also be integrated into the HPE Ezmeral Data Fabric to enable simplified management and data insights for the services. All the Mo SMB services can be stopped, started or restarted at the same time or individually using the HPE Ezmeral Data Fabric Control System. The metrics for the Mo SMB services could also be viewed using Grafana, which can be launched from the HPE Ezmeral Data Fabric Control System.

Integration for management of the Mo SMB services from MCS via warden. Detailed steps that were performed on all HPE Ezmeral Data Fabric nodes to integrate the Mo SMB services into HPE Ezmeral Data Fabric are listed below. I. Add the following entry to the end of /etc/sudoers:
# cat /etc/sudoers mapr ALL=(ALL) NOPASSWD: ALL

# cd /opt/mapr/roles/ # touch mosmb # touch mosmb-zookeeper # ls apiserver fileserver grafana hbase mastgateway s3server cldb fluentd hadoop-client hbaserest mosmb zookeeper collectd gateway hadoop-util kibana mosmb-zookeeper

III. Create scripts for the mosmb and mosmb-zookeeper services in /opt/mapr/initscripts/mosmb.service and /opt/mapr/initscripts/mosmb-zookeeper.service ( NOTE : plz refer to the link provided for sample scripts at the bottom of this page)
# chmod +x /opt/mapr/initscripts/mosmb* # ls -la /opt/mapr/initscripts/mosmb* -rwxr-xr-x 1 root root 526 Aug 18 10:06 mosmb.service -rwxr-xr-x 1 root root 828 Aug 18 10:10 mosmb-zookeeper.service

V. Create Warden scripts for the Mo SMB services in /opt/mapr/conf/conf.d/warden.mosmb.conf and /opt/mapr/conf/conf.d/warden.mosmb-zookeeper.conf ( NOTE : plz refer to the link provided for sample conf files at the bottom of this page) VI. Change the owner and group for these mosmb warden conf files

# chown mapr /opt/mapr/conf/conf.d/warden.mosmb.conf # chgrp mapr /opt/mapr/conf/conf.d/warden.mosmb.conf # chown mapr /opt/mapr/conf/conf.d/warden.mosmb-zookeeper.conf # chgrp mapr /opt/mapr/conf/conf.d/warden.mosmb-zookeeper.conf #ls -la /opt/mapr/conf/conf.d/warden.mosmb* -rw-r--r-- 1 mapr mapr 614 Aug 18 10:19 warden.mosmb.conf -rw-r--r-- 1 mapr mapr 563 Aug 18 10:18 warden.mosmb-zookeeper.conf

All Mo SMB-SA builds are set to use the "Standalone Mode" of operation by default. To use Mo SMB server in Standalone Mode with Kerberos authentication simply follow the configurations mentioned in steps below one by one. 1. Configure Network Time Protocol (NTP) Configuration settings on all Mo SMB server nodes. 2. Install Kerberos packages on Mo SMB server 3. Request the Domain Administration team for AD and DNS Configurations.

Create two-way AD trust between all the required domains if required. (Note: This is not a must). Add conditional forwarders in the DNS manager w.r.t other domains for all domains if required. This is just so that all name resolutions work correctly depending on your IT infrastructure rules and needs. Configure name resolution settings on Mo SMB server. Join the Mo SMB server to the Active Directory Domain Remove restricted Kerberos entries from Active Directory Domain 4.

Kerberos and related services configurations on Mo SMB server machine. For release versions:3.1.1_000 refer - Mo SMB Kerberos Ticket Generation For release versions:3.1.1_001 and above refer - Mo SMB Kerberos Ticket Generation. 5. Mo SMB server now supports NTLMSSP authentication from Release versions:3.1.1_004 and above. For Mo SMB server node, carry out these changes to get the NTLMSSP authentication support working. 7.

Setup the Mo SMB configuration file (/etc/mosmb/mosmb.json) for standalone use. In global Section check enable High Availability is set to no In section node Info Section , give any integer between 1 to 1023 to node Id In section vinode Section check mechanism is set as inode In share Section check parameter is Cluster is set to no

"globalSection": { .... "enableHighAvailability" : "no" .... "nodeInfoSection": { "nodeId": 1 } "vinodeSection" : { "mechanism" : "inode", .... } "sharesSection": [ { "shareName": "Local Share", "comment": "File Share Directory", "sharePath": "/opt/MoSMB_share", "isContinouslyAvailable": "yes", "isCluster": "no", ....

8. If the server machines are centos(or similar) 8 and above supported encryption options needs to be enabled from server side as well in order for Kerberos authentication to work with RC4. On every Cent OS 8.3 and above Mo SMB server, where authentication against AD Domain Controllers takes place, use the update-crypto-policies command to enable the AD-SUPPORT cryptographic subpolicy in addition to the DEFAULT cryptographic policy.

Command : update-crypto-policies --set DEFAULT:AD-SUPPORT Output : Setting system policy to DEFAULT:AD-SUPPORT Command : init 6 Notes : 1. System-wide crypto policies are applied on application start-up so it is recommended to restart the system for the change of policies to fully take place. 3. The AD-SUPPORT cryptographic subpolicy is only available on CentOS 8.3 and newer.

To enable support for RC4 in Cent OS 8.2 , create and enable a custom cryptographic module policy with cipher = RC4-128+. For more details, see Customizing system-wide cryptographic policies with policy modifiers. Reference links :
To enable support for RC4 in Cent OS 8.0 and Cent OS 8.1 , add +rc4 to the permitted_enctypes option in the /etc/crypto-policies/back-ends/krb5.config file
[libdefaults] permitted_enctypes = aes256-cts-hmac-sha1-96 aes256-cts-hmac-sha384-192 camellia256-cts-cmac aes128-cts-hmac-sha1-96 aes128-cts-hmac-sha256-128 camellia128-cts-cmac +rc4
[provider_sect] default = default_sect [default_sect] # activate = 1
[provider_sect] default = default_sect legacy = legacy_sect [openssl_init] providers = provider_sect [default_sect] activate = 1 [legacy_sect] activate = 1
10. Start Mo SMB service and check status service mosmb status and check the mosmb.log for details. You can check details of Mo SMB server Maintenance here

1 Integration with Prometheus to monitor Mo SMB statistics 1.1 Prometheus server installation, configuration & monitoring of metrics on that server 1.2 'Node Exporter' installation and using it to send the metrics from Prometheus Target Machine 1.3 How to see the data through a browser on the Prometheus server side 1.4 Integration with Grafana 1.5 Creating a New Dashboard in Grafana 1.1 Prometheus server installation, configuration & monitoring of metrics on that server 1.2 'Node Exporter' installation and using it to send the metrics from Prometheus Target Machine 1.3 How to see the data through a browser on the Prometheus server side

Prometheus is an open-source systems monitoring toolkit that helps to monitor an IT infrastructure, including: Servers, Networks etc. It can be configured with the Mo SMB to get Mo SMB statistics and Mo SMB node's metrics like disk usage, I/O operations etc. Prometheus has two major components : Prometheus server & Prometheus targets (where node exporter is installed). NOTE: This integration is available in Mo SMB from version 093 onwards
On Ubuntu, use below command sudo apt install prometheus OR For redhat/CentOS Download the prometheus package from the link below and "untar" it. Ref: https://prometheus.io/download/
If package is installed through package manager then "/etc/prometheus/prometheus.yml" is a prometheus server configuration file.
If a package tar file is downloaded and installed then "prometheus.yml" is a prometheus server configuration file in the local directory where the package is untarred.
In the configuration file, there is a "static_configs" label which is under the "scrape_configs" label. Need to add the targets of which the prometheus needs to scrape the metrics.
E.g. scrape_configs: static_configs: - targets: ['10.11.1.167:9090']
sudo systemctl start/stop prometheus OR /usr/bin/prometheus --config.file=<path to PROMETHEUS_CONFIG_FILE> E.g. /usr/bin/prometheus --config.file=/etc/prometheus/prometheus.yml

# Download the "node_exporter" for the Target Machine from https://github.com/prometheus/node_exporter/releases # Untar the downloaded package of "node_exporter". # Get into the directory and start the node_exporter as follows ./node_exporter --web.listen-address <TARGET_MACHINE>:<PORT_NUMBER> --collector.textfile.directory=<DIR_WHERE_METRICS_DUMPED> e.g. /node_exporter --web.listen-address 10.11.1.167:9090 --collector.textfile.directory=/var/lib/node_exporter Note : "--collector.textfile.directory" option is where the <FILE_NAME>.prom needs to be created. In case of the  MoSMB stats", the file "processed_stats.prom" gets created by  cmdpipe  but the path of the  DIR_WHERE_METRICS_DUMPED  (mentioned as option to node_exporter) should be same as that of the path given to the  cmdpipe  during statistics generation.
The metric data is dumped in "key:value pair" format in the " processed_stats.prom " and the "key:value" pair gets updated in every iteration after the -i interval mentioned in cmdpipe command.

# Go to http://<IP_ADDRESS_OF_GRAFANA>:3000 E.g. http://10.11.0.102:3000 # Go to Connections => menu and select Connections => Add new connection or Data Sources . # Search for and select Prometheus as the data source type # Select   add new datasource  . Give a name to this data source, add prometheus server url " http://<PROMETHEUS_SERVER>:9090 " # Keep rest as default and click "Save and Test" to confirm Grafana can communicate with Prometheus.

Add Dashboard: On the left-hand menu, select Dashboard. Click  New  and add New Dashboard Add Visualization: Click Add Visualization to your dashboard. A panel will appear on the screen, ensure your newly added Prometheus data source is selected. Query Tab: Within the new panel, go to the Query tab. In the Query field, Select the Metric and type an expression to retrieve the desired metric from Prometheus, for example Mo SMB_Storage Read Latency_msec .

Clicking Run queries will give you the graph for this metric Choose Visualization: Select your desired visualization type, such as a Time series graph on the right-hand side of the panel. Name the panel. Save the Dashboard.

Save: Click the Save icon in the top-right corner to save your dashboard and the new graph panel Navigate to Dashboard and select your dashboard and click on  explore  Or click on "Explore" on the left-hand side and select the desired data source name from the drop down list and run queries as per requirement.

Your new panel will now display the metrics data from Prometheus in a visual graph within your Grafana Dashboard Grafana: Build Query by selecting a Metric and use it to see Graph Grafana: Build Query by selecting a Metric and use it to see Graph

Mo SMB provides users with a product key to activate the Mo SMB product. Online: For Mo SMB servers with an active network connection. Offline: For Mo SMB servers that do not have any active network connection. To start the Mo SMB server you require a trial license or a product key. Note : If the Mo SMB server is a GCP instance then the following licensing scheme is not applicable.

Verify if the Mo SMB server machine has a working and active internet connection. Verify if all network adapters of Mo SMB server's are up. Verify if the firewall policies allow port 443 to connect to the Mo SMB license servers. Verify if the time and date settings of the Mo SMB server are correct or up-to-date. Verify if https://wyday.com/limelm/ is reachable from the Mo SMB server.
Mo SMB checks license validity in certain time period for online activation Mo SMB server must always be able to communicate with https://wyday.com/limelm

Verify if the trialrequest.xml or request.xml is generated on the same Mo SMB server machine on which you need to activate the Mo SMB license. Verify if the trialresponse.xml or response.xml file provided to you is placed at the /etc/mosmb/license/ location. Verify if the product key mentioned in the Mo SMB configuration and the product key used while activating the license is the same.

3 Online Activation of the Product 3.1 Trial License Key 3.2 Trial Extension Key 3.3 Product Key 4 Offline Activation of the Product 4.1 Trial/Trial Extension License Key 4.2 Product Key 5 Applying changes made to a product key - offline

In the case of a trial license the Product key parameter in /etc/mosmb/mosmb.json file should be empty - "product Key": "" Restart the Mo SMB service for the changes made in Mo SMB configuration file to take effect. Note : The Trial license will be activated only once per server. After expiry contact the Mo SMB support

Contact the Mo SMB support support@ryussi.com for a trial extension key. In the /etc/mosmb/mosmb.json file in the Product key section enter the trial extension key. Restart the Mo SMB service for the changes made in Mo SMB configuration file to take effect. Note : Make sure you remove all the previous trial Request.xml and trial Response.xml files from the /etc/mosmb/license folder

Contact the Mo SMB support support@ryussi.com to purchase the product key. In the /etc/mosmb/mosmb.json file in the Product key section enter the product key provided. For the changes made in the Mo SMB configuration file to take effect Restart the Mo SMB service. Note : Make sure you remove all the previous trial Request.xml and trial Response.xml files if at all they exist from previous offline activations, from the /etc/mosmb/license folder

In the case of a trial license the Product key parameter in the /etc/mosmb/mosmb.json file should be empty - "product Key": "" This binary will generate a trial Request.xml file at the /etc/mosmb/license/ location. Send this file to the Mo SMB support support@ryussi.com and we will share a response trial Response.xml file. Copy this trial Response.xml file at the /etc/mosmb/license/ location. Restart the Mo SMB service for the changes to take effect.
Note : The Trial license will be activated only once per machine. After expiry contact the Mo SMB support support@ryussi.com for the Trial Extension Key or to purchase the Product Key.

Contact the Mo SMB support support@ryussi.com to purchase the product key. Run the /etc/mosmb/license/mosmb_offline_license binary as follows: /etc/mosmb/license/mosmb_offline_license <Product Key> The Product Key should be the one mentioned in the /etc/mosmb/mosmb.json file. This binary will generate a request.xml file at the /etc/mosmb/license/ location. Send this file to Mo SMB support support@ryussi.com and we will share a response.xml response file.
Copy the response.xml file to the /etc/mosmb/license/ location. Restart the Mo SMB service for the changes to take effect.

Note :Make sure you remove all the previous trial Request.xml and trial Response.xml files from the /etc/mosmb/license folder Alternatively , for a cluster setup, which is set to use clush (clustershell), mosmbcli get-req and deploy-resp options can be used to get the request.xml files from all nodes and put in a tar file which can be then emailed to the Mo SMB support support@ryussi.com

If old request.xml exists in the license folder (/etc/mosmb/license/) then we use that to generate new responses If old request.xml does not exist in the license folder, and license is still valid, to generate request.xml, we need to deactivate first (request.xml is not generated if product is activated) /etc/mosmb/license/.mosmb_license_deactivate Run the /etc/mosmb/license/mosmb_offline_license binary as follows: etc/mosmb/license/mosmb_offline_license <Product Key> The Product Key should be the one mentioned in the /etc/mosmb/mosmb.json file.

This binary will generate a request.xml file at the /etc/mosmb/license/ location. Send the request.xml file to Mo SMB support support@ryussi.com and we will share a response.xml response file. Copy the response.xml file to the /etc/mosmb/license/ location. if product is already deactivated, Restart the Mo SMB service for the license changes to take effect. if old request was used, then deactivate now.. and then restart service
If license is still valid, we need to deactivate first /etc/mosmb/license/.mosmb_license_deactivate Then restart mosmb service for the new activation to take effect

Hyper V VM creation is currently only supported in the krb5 authentication mode and the posix acl authorization mode configuration. Verify if all of the following settings are done: The Mo SMB share must have the complete permissions set for all. (777 -- drwxrwxrwx) The Mo SMB share Ownership Settings must be carried out. The File Creation Mask and Directory Creation Mask are set to 0777 in the Mo SMB Share Configuration .

Sign in to the Hyper V machine with the Domain Administrator user credentials. The Hyper V machine's primary group must be the same as the Mo SMB share owner and it must be the Domain Admin. Note : The Domain Admin requirement depends on the Hyper V configuration settings. If a non-admin user has permissions to create the Hyper V VM then this user can create the VM on the Mo SMB share - if the share settings are configured for that non-admin user.

To scale up the Mo SMB on one node, add more NICs, CPU or memory as desired. If the backend storage is a bottleneck, add high performance storage like NVMe disks. To scale out, add another Mo SMB node to the cluster and expose the same share.

For detailed information please refer Performance Tuning pages Note: For TCP For a given NIC, set minimum values if you want Mo SMB to use low RAM and CPU cores For a given NIC, set maximum values if you want Mo SMB to use higher RAM and CPU cores For a given NIC - Keep the TCP buffers:thread ratio approximately as given in the above column for e.g.

if you increase the no of threads by 1 for 10Gbps bw then increase the ringbuffer Count by 8 (ringbuffer Count   preprocessor threads 8   8=64 TCP buffers) Set Transaction Size=8 for large files transactions Set Transaction Size=1 for small file io transactions Note: For RDMA + TCP thread Pool Size is common to both RDMA and TCP transport.

Use these logs for Security Monitoring. One can set the audit module s log level in the /etc/mosmb/mosmb_logging.conf file to 2 to get detailed audit logs . Set the audit log level in the /etc/mosmb/mosmb_logging.conf file to 3. AUDIT_L = 3 Restart the Mo SMB service OR update logging at runtime without restarting mosmb The mosmb_audit.log audit log file will then be generated at the log location mentioned in the mosmb.json file, default is /var/log .

Audit logs are populated for following categories: CREATE RENAME DELETE PERMISSION_CHANGE LOGIN LOGOFF When Mo SMB is installed, the scripts for the log rotation of the mosmb audit logs are added in the /etc/cron.hourly directory (mologrotate) and the /etc/logrotate.d directory. Logs are rotated when the log file size reaches 500 MB. The log size threshold reached is checked on an hourly basis. To disable the audit logs set the audit log level to 0.

(ERROR level) AUDIT_L = 0 Restart the Mo SMB service OR update logging at runtime without restarting mosmb Note that : Along with mosmb_audit.log other log files are rotated in similar way; with files mosmbauditlr (mosmb_audit.log), mosmblr (mosmb.log) and zookeeperlr (mosmb_vinode.log) present under /etc/logrotate.d/

In Cent OS 8.* , RC4 encryption is disabled in the DEFAULT crypto policy level. Following changes are needed for kerberos to work on Cent OS 8* There are two solutions (To maintain stronger security, we suggest you use first option) In AD, go to Users and Computers -> Users ->Select the special user-> Open properties ->select account tab, and choose the following account options This account supports Kerberos AES 128 bit encryption This account supports Kerberos AES 256 bit encryption 2.

On mosmb server make changes to DEFAULT crypto policy, to include RC4 i) On every Cent OS 8.3 and above mosmb server, where authentication against AD Domain Controllers takes place, Use the update-crypto-policies command to enable the AD-SUPPORT cryptographic subpolicy in addition to the DEFAULT cryptographic policy.

[root@host ~]# update-crypto-policies --set DEFAULT:AD-SUPPORT Setting system policy to DEFAULT:AD-SUPPORT Note: System-wide crypto policies are applied on application start-up. It is recommended to restart the system for the change of policies to fully take place.

The AD-SUPPORT cryptographic subpolicy is only available on Cent OS 8.3 and newer. ii) To enable support for RC4 in Cent OS 8.2 , create and enable a custom cryptographic module policy with cipher = RC4-128+. For more details, see Customizing system-wide cryptographic policies with policy modifiers. iii) To enable support for RC4 in Cent OS 8.0 and Cent OS 8.1 , add +rc4 to the permitted_enctypes option in the /etc/crypto-policies/back-ends/krb5.config file
[libdefaults] permitted_enctypes = aes256-cts-hmac-sha1-96 aes256-cts-hmac-sha384-192 camellia256-cts-cmac aes128-cts-hmac-sha1-96 aes128-cts-hmac-sha256-128 camellia128-cts-cmac +rc4

In a situation, where one wants to change the node_id of a node, from a cluster, it is recommended that full cluster is stopped (Mo SMB and Zookeeper both) and a clean up is carried out, before you change the node_id of a node or many nodes in the mosmb.json.

1. stop mosmb service on all nodes 2. stop mosmb-zookeeper on all zookeeper nodes 3. run the clean up command /etc/mosmb/scripts/mosmb_zk-cleanup.sh <zookeeper install directory path> e.g. /etc/mosmb/scripts/mosmb_zk-cleanup.sh /opt/mosmb/zookeeper/apache-zookeeper-3.6.3-bin 4. once the clean up is finished, open /etc/mosmb/mosmb.json of the node where you want to change the node_id, and update it. (one node or many nodes of cluster as per your requirement) 5. Save the json files and restart the cluster, first all zk servers and then all mosmb services in the cluster nodes
Mo SMB supports Microsoft Active Directory (AD) based Kerberos authentication. The Mo SMB server is added to the AD Domain to authenticate and authorize the SMB Client's access to Mo SMB shares.

You must have a working model of the AD in your Evaluation and/or Production environment before you start using the appliance with kerberos authentication. You require Active Directory Domain Administrator user to make related modifications. You can use any one of the following utility to join the Mo SMB server to AD.

Install the realm and dependent packages on the server machine. Command : yum install adcli OR apt-get install adcli To join the domain with adcli: Command : adcli join --service-name=host -D <AD_domain_name> -U <AD_domain_administrator_user> --host-fqdn= <server_hostname>.<AD_domain_name> Example : adcli join --service-name=host -D MOSMBAD.DOM -U user1@MOSMBAD.DOM --host-fqdn=mymosmbserver.mosmbad.dom Password for user1@MOSMBAD.DOM: Note: The domain join creates a machine entry in the AD.

You must update the DNS entries for forward lookup zone and reverse lookup zone manually. To leave a domain with adcli: Command : adcli delete-computer --domain= <AD_domain_name> -U <AD_domain_administrator_user> Note: This deletes the entry from AD. You must update the DNS entries for forward lookup zone and reverse lookup zone manually.

Install the realm and dependent packages on the server machine. Command : yum install realmd OR apt-get install realm Search for the domain you wish to join. Command : realm discover <domain name> Note: <domain name> here is the search value from the /etc/resolv.conf file, that is ad.example.com. To join the domain with realm.

Command : realm join --user= <AD_domain_administrator_user> <AD_domain_name> Example : realm join --user=user1 mosmbad.dom Password for user1: Note: The domain join creates a machine entry in the AD. You must update the DNS entries for forward lookup zone and reverse lookup zone manually. To leave the domain with realm. Command : realm leave <AD_domain_name> The above mentioned methods of AD joining have been provided solely to guide you.

MIT kerberos package must be installed on the Mo SMB server machine. The krb5-workstation/krb5-user package contains the basic Kerberos programs (kinit, klist, kdestroy, kpasswd). If your network uses Kerberos, this package should be installed on every workstation. Search and install the Kerberos package on Cent OS:
Command 1 : yum search kerberos Output 1 : .... krb5-workstation.x86_64 : Kerberos 5 programs for use on workstations Command 2 : yum install krb5-workstation.x86_64
Command 1 : apt-cache search krb5 Output 1 : .... krb5-user - basic programs to authenticate using MIT Kerberos Command 2 : apt-get install krb5-user
Command 1 : zypper search krb5-client Output 1 : .... krb5-client | Client programs of the MIT Kerberos5 implementation | package Command 2 : zypper install krb5-client

For Cluster builds, a zookeeper rpm will also be provided along with MoSMB rpm. MoSMB-zookeeper rpm is present at : /opt/mosmb/package/mosmb_install To install you can use following command: rpm -ivh /opt/mosmb/package/mosmb_install/mosmb-zookeeper-3.6.3-1.x86_64.rpm Verifying... ################################# [100%] Preparing... ################################# [100%] Updating / installing... 1:mosmb-zookeeper-3.6.3-1 ################################# [100%] zookeeper is installed at : /opt/mosmb/zookeeper/
zookeeper will be installed at /opt/mosmb/zookeeper/ and user will need to make appropriate changes to zoo.cfg and then start zk Server as mentioned in above steps.
1. Go to other extracted directory apache-zookeeper-3.6.3-bin 2. Copy sample Zoo Keeper configuration file from /etc/mosmb/.

For detailed information on the configuration parameters refer to the Zoo Keeper Server Configuration Parameters page. 6. Create the myid file on the Zoo Keeper server/s at the /var/lib/zookeeper-mosmb/ path.

Test data : ZooKeeper Server IP list - 10.10.0.1, 10.10.0.2, 10.10.0.3 File on server 1 (10.10.0.1) : cat /var/lib/zookeeper-mosmb/myid Output on server 1 (10.10.0.1) : 1 File on server 2 (10.10.0.2) : cat /var/lib/zookeeper-mosmb/myid Output on server 2 (10.10.0.2) : 2 File on server 3 (10.10.0.3) : cat /var/lib/zookeeper-mosmb/myid Output on server 3 (10.10.0.3) : 3

This step is not mandatory. In case zookeeper restarts, the server side log located in the log folder of zookeeper installation path, (/opt/mosmb/zookeeper/apache-zookeeper-3.6.3-bin/logs/) is overwritten and old logs are over-written/lost. To avoid loss of old logs, one can do the following 1. A solution is to modify zk Env.sh and set ZOO_LOG4J_PROP
$ cat /opt/mosmb/zookeeper/apache-zookeeper-3.6.3-bin/bin/zkEnv.sh | grep -i PROP if [ "x${ZOO_LOG4J_PROP}" = "x" ] ZOO_LOG4J_PROP="INFO,CONSOLE"
$ cat /opt/mosmb/zookeeper/apache-zookeeper-3.6.3-bin/bin/zkEnv.sh | grep -i PROP if [ "x${ZOO_LOG4J_PROP}" = "x" ] ZOO_LOG4J_PROP="INFO,CONSOLE, ROLLINGFILE "
3. Now every restart of mosmb-zookeeper service will have two log files, one current (.out) and one backed up with old logs (.log).
This will create a Zoo Keeper log file at location ../apache-zookeeper-3.6.3-bin/logs/ that will contain the Zoo Keeper server related logs. 2. Check status.
Command : ./bin/zkServer.sh status Output : JMX enabled by default Using config: ../apache-zookeeper-3.6.3-bin/conf/zoo.cfg Mode: Standalone

Test data : ZooKeeper Server IP list - 10.10.0.1, 10.10.0.2, 10.10.0.3 Command : ./bin/zkServer.sh status Output : JMX enabled by default Using config: ../apache-zookeeper-3.6.3-bin/conf/zoo.cfg' Mode: Leader (This output will be different on all server one will be the leader and other servers will be Follower )

All Mo SMB-CL builds supports multi domain clustering. To form a multi domain Mo SMB cluster simply follow the configurations mentioned in steps below one by one. 1. Carry out Network Time Protocol (NTP) Configuration settings on all Mo SMB server nodes. 2. Install Kerberos packages on all Mo SMB server nodes 3. Request the Domain Administration team for AD and DNS Configurations. Add conditional forwarders in the DNS manager w.r.t other domains.

Create a Special user in the Active Directory (AD) (Step required to be carried out only once per Mo SMB cluster) Create Mo SMB Cluster Keytab (Step required to be carried out only once per Mo SMB cluster) Carry out name resolution settings on all Mo SMB servers nodes. 4. Kerberos and related services configurations on all Mo SMB server nodes in the cluster.

For release versions:3.1.1_000 refer - Mo SMB Kerberos Ticket Generation For release versions:3.1.1_001 and above refer - Mo SMB Kerberos Ticket Generation. 5. Mo SMB server now supports NTLMSSP authentication from Release versions:3.1.1_004 and above. For each Mo SMB server node in cluster, carry out these changes to get the NTLMSSP authentication support working. 6. Mo SMB server is Zoo Keeper client; Perform Mo SMB -Zoo Keeper settings 8.

Setup the Mo SMB configuration file (/etc/mosmb/mosmb.json) for cluster use. In global Section set enable High Availability to yes In persistent Store Section keep pstore Path location on the common storage path on all Mo SMB nodes in the cluster. In section node Info Section , assign a different node Id to each node in Mo SMB cluster. In section auth Section , set auth Mode as ntlm Local .

(NOTE: This will ensure krb5 authentication is tried first, if that is not successful, the new releases support ntlm SSP authentication, if that fails then ntlm Local authentication is tried.) In share Section keep share Path location on the common storage path on all Mo SMB nodes in the cluster. In share Section set parameters is Continously Available and is Cluster to yes

"globalSection": { .... "enableHighAvailability" : "yes" .... "persistentStoreSection": { "pstorePath": "/mnt/mycluster/MoSMB_mhk", .... "nodeInfoSection": { "nodeId": 1 } "authSection": { "authMode": "ntlmLocal" } "sharesSection": [ { "shareName": "Cluster Share", "comment": "File Share Directory", "sharePath": "/mnt/mycluster/MoSMB_share", "isContinouslyAvailable": "yes", "isCluster": "yes", ....

On every Cent OS 8.3 and above Mo SMB server node, where authentication against AD Domain Controllers takes place, use the update-crypto-policies command to enable the AD-SUPPORT cryptographic subpolicy in addition to the DEFAULT cryptographic policy.

Command : update-crypto-policies --set DEFAULT:AD-SUPPORT Output : Setting system policy to DEFAULT:AD-SUPPORT Command : init 6 Notes : 1. System-wide crypto policies are applied on application start-up so it is recommended to restart the system for the change of policies to fully take place. 3. The AD-SUPPORT cryptographic subpolicy is only available on CentOS 8.3 and newer.

In the Windows world, permissions are more granular than Linux. Each file system object is associated with multiple users/groups. Each of which may contain around fourteen/fifteen permissions. When file object is accessed by both the world Linux and Windows then to maintain compatibility and consistency, mapping needs to be done. The mapping is challenging as in Linux only three permissions are defined vs fifteen in windows as seen below.

Example:  Setting Extended ACEs - setfacl -m u:<domain user>:<permissions>,g:<domain group>:<permissions>,o::<permissions> <absolute path of file/folder> Setting Extended ACEs recursively - setfacl -R -m u:<domain user>:<permissions>,g:<domain group>:<permissions>,o::<permissions> <absolute path of file/folder> Setting default/Inherited ACEs - setfacl -d -m u:<domain user>:<permissions>,g:<domain group>:<permissions>,o::<permissions> <absolute path of folder> Setting default/Inherited ACEs recursively - setfacl -R -d -m u:<domain user>:<permissions>,g:<domain group>:<permissions>,o::<permissions> <absolute path of file/folder>

Linux filesystem allows only Owner and superusers (sudo) to set or modify permissions of files and folders. In Linux world  owner ,  primary group  and  other  can not be removed, though all permissions can be removed for the same except for the owner. Read permissions for the owner can not be removed.
Example:  file/folder can have Owner:Group:Other permissions to any combination of rwx but lowest it can be set to as r--:---:---

All files and folders in the share have Inheritance enabled by default. All inherited permissions are by default applied as This folder subfolder and files . Permissions will be inherited on basis of applies to section mentioned while setting new permissions or inheriting existing as follows,

On any share inheritance is always enabled. Disable inheritance button on windows client (Properties   security   Advance   Disable Inheritance) is not supported. Only Allow ACE type is supported. Deny type from windows property tab is not supported. Windows system ACL permission requests will not be supported. Only Owner and super user is allowed to add new permissions, modify newly added permissions or remove newly added permissions on shares, files and folders.

Recommended to add permissions in default ACEs from server side on shared path for inheritance to be enabled. When existing ACEs permissions on share root are modified; it will remove the inherited(default) permissions of that specific shared path. The user will be warned about this. This behavior is in adherence to Windows smb share.

Behavior of checkbox - Only apply these permissions to objects and/or containers within this container - When any new user/group permission is added with this checkbox selected then it will be applied as "This folder only" for the current folder and to it's existing immediate children as "This folder only" Special case - EVERYONE SID We give inherited permissions higher priority over Everyone when Everyone has lower permissions than inherited permissions then only inherited permissions will be considered.

It is not recommended to mix linux local users or root with AD users/groups in permissions - Linux local users/groups will be displayed as linux UIDs/GIDs on windows clients under security tab display. When Owner and Group is set to root:root on share root then any user won't be able to modify the permissions also others will not be able to access file/folder using rwx permission, as it is not allowed on linux.

Linux local users/groups will be displayed as linux UIDs/GIDs on windows clients under security tab display. When Owner and Group is set to root:root on share root then any user won't be able to modify the permissions also others will not be able to access file/folder using rwx permission, as it is not allowed on linux. Access time mask or acl mask is to be kept as rwx always (mask::rwx & default:mask::rwx), as permissions seen on windows client side are not filtered.

Please not that In case of inherited shares - creation time mask is ignored and the permissions are applied as per the they are inherited and acl mask is set to rwx In case of non inherited shares - creation time mask is considered and acl mask is set to rwx In case of inherited shares - creation time mask is ignored and the permissions are applied as per the they are inherited and acl mask is set to rwx In case of non inherited shares - creation time mask is considered and acl mask is set to rwx Advance Posix ACL module supports multiple group mapping for any user up to 256 groups.

The node on which ansible is installed is called ' ansible control node' . The nodes on which ansible will perform installation and configuration are called as ' ansible client nodes' (eg. mosmb,zookeeper installation). Playbook is the main script which triggers the installation. It is advisable to keep the ansible control node different from ansible client nodes, but you can use one of the ansible client node as ansible control node.
This installer can be used to install Mo SMB on a fleet of VM's which can be located either on prem or on the cloud. The installer is split into 3 playbooks and 6 roles.

Roles are nothing but individual task entities. Currently there are 6 roles inside the roles folder. The final preinstall role is consumed by mapr installer to trigger mosmb installation either before or after mapr installation. If we want to install mosmb after mapr installation then this directory needs to be renamed to postinstall. Tasks Completed by each playbook

Configure DNS entries for individual nodes as well as for a common cluster name. Create a service account and generate keytab against common cluster name with this account. Edit resolv.conf so that the node can locate nameservers and active directory realm's.

Currently maprfs is the only storage that can be configured. For mapr role, if the node to be setup is an outside mapr cluster node then we perform some extra steps like adding mapr repo, installing mapr client to be able to generate mapr service ticket. This steps are skipped if the node is itself one of the mapr node. Following are the common steps for both scenarios.

A domain admin user is also required by ansible to communicate with the domain controller. Ansible uses Win RM for communicating with remote windows clients. Therefore you need to run the following two scripts for setting Win RM up on you remote windows machine (for us it is the Domain Controller). According to ansible documentation, there is bug in Win RM service that limits the amount of memory available to run Win RM scripts.

$url = " https://raw.githubusercontent.com/jborean93/ansible-windows/master/scripts/Install-WMF3Hotfix.ps1 " $file = "$env:temp\Install-WMF3Hotfix.ps1" (New-Object -TypeName System.Net.WebClient).DownloadFile($url, $file) powershell.exe -ExecutionPolicy ByPass -File $file -Verbose

NOTE : If you face issues/errors in above step, one can try raw copy of the script file and run that. This powershell script sets up the Win RM service so that ansible can communicate with it over HTTPS.

$url = " https://github.com/ansible/ansible-documentation/blob/devel/examples/scripts/ConfigureRemotingForAnsible.ps1 " $file = "$env:temp\ConfigureRemotingForAnsible.ps1" (New-Object -TypeName System.Net.WebClient).DownloadFile($url, $file) powershell.exe -ExecutionPolicy ByPass -File $file

All Mo SMB-CL builds supports single domain clustering. To form a single domain Mo SMB cluster simply follow the configurations mentioned in steps below one by one. 1. Carry out Network Time Protocol (NTP) Configuration settings on all Mo SMB server nodes. 2. Install Kerberos packages on all Mo SMB server nodes 3. Request the Domain Administration team for AD and DNS Configurations.

Create a Special user in the Active Directory (AD) (Step required to be carried out only once per Mo SMB cluster) Create Mo SMB Cluster Keytab (Step required to be carried out only once per Mo SMB cluster) Copy generated keytab file to all the Mo SMB server nodes which are needed in a cluster as mosmb.keytab at location /etc/mosmb/ Carry out name resolution settings on all Mo SMB servers nodes. 4. Kerberos and related services configurations on all Mo SMB server nodes in the cluster.

For release versions:3.1.1_000 refer - Mo SMB Kerberos Ticket Generation For release versions:3.1.1_001 and above refer - Mo SMB Kerberos Ticket Generation. 5. Mo SMB server now supports NTLMSSP authentication from Release versions:3.1.1_004 and above. For each Mo SMB server node in cluster, carry out these changes to get the NTLMSSP authentication support working. 6. Mo SMB server is Zoo Keeper client; Perform Mo SMB -Zoo Keeper settings 8.

Setup the Mo SMB configuration file (/etc/mosmb/mosmb.json) for cluster use. In global Section set enable High Availability to yes In persistent Store Section keep pstore Path location on the common storage path on all Mo SMB nodes in the cluster. In section node Info Section , assign a different node Id to each node in Mo SMB cluster. In section auth Section , set auth Mode as ntlm Local .

(NOTE: This will ensure krb5 authentication is tried first, if that is not successful, the new releases support ntlm SSP authentication, if that fails then ntlm Local authentication is tried.) In share Section keep share Path location on the common storage path on all Mo SMB nodes in the cluster. In share Section set parameters is Continously Available and is Cluster to yes

"globalSection": { .... "enableHighAvailability" : "yes" .... "persistentStoreSection": { "pstorePath": "/mnt/mycluster/MoSMB_mhk", .... "nodeInfoSection": { "nodeId": 1 } "authSection": { "authMode": "ntlmLocal" } "sharesSection": [ { "shareName": "Cluster Share", "comment": "File Share Directory", "sharePath": "/mnt/mycluster/MoSMB_share", "isContinouslyAvailable": "yes", "isCluster": "yes", ....

9. If the server machines are centos(or similar) 8 and above supported encryption options needs to be enabled from server side as well in order for Kerberos authentication to work completely. On every Cent OS 8.3 and above Mo SMB server node, where authentication against AD Domain Controllers takes place, use the update-crypto-policies command to enable the AD-SUPPORT cryptographic subpolicy in addition to the DEFAULT cryptographic policy.

Command : update-crypto-policies --set DEFAULT:AD-SUPPORT Output : Setting system policy to DEFAULT:AD-SUPPORT Command : init 6 Notes : 1. System-wide crypto policies are applied on application start-up so it is recommended to restart the system for the change of policies to fully take place. 3. The AD-SUPPORT cryptographic subpolicy is only available on CentOS 8.3 and newer.

Please note that the performance tuning recommendations provided in this guide are general guidelines. Optimal configuration for your environment may differ depending on your workload, hardware, and other factors. To achieve the best results, it's recommended that you conduct performance tests on your specific configuration and make adjustments as necessary.

1 Performance tuning of server level parameters 1.1 Global level parameters in the /etc/mosmb/mosmb.json file 1.2 Mo SMB Logging /etc/mosmb/mosmb_logging.conf Configurations 1.1 Global level parameters in the /etc/mosmb/mosmb.json file

The parameter settings in the /etc/mosmb/mosmb.json and /etc/mosmb/mosmb_logging.conf file will help set the performance of the Mo SMB server. Note: Any changes to /etc/mosmb/mosmb.json mandates restarting the Mo SMB server.

"max Protocol": "smb302"   Set this to smb302. Setting max protocol lower than this can give low performance. "required Message Signing": "no"   Set required signing to  no  to give better performance. This will not disable the signing completely. When the client sends a signed request the Mo SMB server will send the signed response.

For more information refer to Microsoft Network Server: Digitally Sign Communications For high security reasons you may want to all SMB clients to sign each and every packet, then you can set this to  yes . This security feature can have some cost on performance. "global Encrypt Data": "no"   Set this parameter to  no  to give better performance. For very high security reasons you may want to set all SMB clients to encrypt each and every packet, then you can set this to  yes .

This security feature can have cost on performance. "share Level Encryption": "no"   Set this parameter to  no  to give better performance. For very high security reasons you may want to set all SMB clients accessing this share to encrypt each and every packet, then you can set this to  yes . This security feature can have cost on performance. "enable Multi Channel": "yes"   Set this parameter to  yes .

If there are multiple NICs in the server and an SMB client is accessible via both the NICs then the bandwidth will get aggregated and provide HA in case of any NIC failure. In case there are multiple network paths between the client and the server, the SMB3 protocol uses the multi-channel feature. "max Transaction Size": 8   Set this parameter to 8 if the use case is to support large data i/o to get good performance.

Set this parameter to 1 if the use case is to support small data i/o to get good performance. "max Read Size": 8   Set this parameter to 8 if the use case is to support large data i/o to get good performance. Set this parameter to 1 if the use case is to support small data i/o to get good performance. "max Write Size": 8   Set this parameter to 8 if the use case is to support large data i/o to get good performance.

Set this parameter to 1 if the use case is to support small data i/o to get good performance. "verification Level": "low"   Set this parameter to  low  for good performance. For security reasons you can set this to  high . In this case, Mo SMB server will perform all checks to verify each and every incoming packet prior to processing. "enable Byte Range Lock": "no"   Set this parameter to  no . For use cases like SQL database etc., set this to  yes . This will have an impact on performance.

"bind Interface": "network interface name    Specify  the network interface(s)  over which the SMB client is going to connect. This is an important setting for performance. "smb Direct Ring": 512   This is an RDMA parameter that allocates and reserves the specified number of buffers per RDMA connection. RDMA performance can be increased by increasing this to 1024, 2048, etc.

"pstore Path": "/opt/mosmb"   pstore Path must never be a directory or sub-directory within a share "max File Path Len": 1   Maximum File Path Length for any file which will get opened through server share. Above parameters tune the resource usage of Mo SMB server and hence the performance of Mo SMB. For a server, based on the CPU cores and RAM we have provided a script to help suggest the baseline numbers.

Mo SMB pre-allocates buffers and the memory utilization is dependent on the following formula: pre Processor Threads   ring Buffer Count   Transaction size Refer to Performance Tuning numbers table for full understanding.
Set the Mo SMB log level to 0 or 1 in the /etc/mosmb/mosmb_logging.conf file for all modules.

Mo SMB provides a performance tuning script on Mo SMB installation. Script is located at /etc/mosmb/scripts/perf_tuning.sh. One can run this script w.r.t the Mo SMB features enabled either TCP or smb Direct. Just run the script to get tuning parameters like pre Processor Threads, thread Pool Size and ring Buffer Count are set as per the server configuration.

This script is used to auto tune the performance parameters of MoSMB configuration based on the cpu and ram available on the server machine for both TCP and smbDirect options. This script will provide suggestions for config tuning parameters for a given number of CPU cores and RAM if provided as parameters. By default, it takes the machine CPU cores and RAM and suggests config parameters.

perf_tuning.sh [[tcp|smbdirect] [-y] [cpu=<n> ram=<n<k|m|g>>]|[-h|--help]] perf_tuning.sh cpu=2 ram=4194304k / 4096m / 4g => Outputs parameters for 2 cpus and 4GB RAM perf_tuning.sh tcp => Outputs parameters for tcp only configuration perf_tuning.sh smbdirect => Outputs parameters for smbDirect configuration cpu = Number of CPUs (Default: system CPUs) ram = Mention RAM size in Kilo/Mega/Gigabytes (Default: System RAM) -y|Y = Answer "yes" for all prompts. -h => shows this help message Note: If CPU and/or RAM parameters are not mentioned, then the script will consider the system CPU cores and system RAM as the default. RAM parameter must be used when the RAM available on the server will be used by other applications and there is a maximum limit on the RAM that must be used by MoSMB server. CPU parameter must be provided when the expected number of cores and CPU time usage by MoSMB server is to be restricted.

# ./perf_tuning.sh tcp Calculating possible performance tuning parameters for TCP Required Message Signing: no Maximum transaction size: 8 MB Ring buffers: 1216 Worker threads: 96 Pre-processor threads: 16 Apply these values to configuration[y/n]:y Applied values to configuration # ./perf_tuning.sh smbdirect Configuration value for "enableSmbDirect" is not set. This would be set to "yes" if configuration is applied Calculating possible performance tuning parameters for TCP and smbDirect Required Message Signing: no Maximum transaction size: 8 MB Ring buffers: 192 Worker threads: 96 Pre-processor threads: 16 smbDirect Buffer size: 1 MB smbDirect ring buffers: 2048 Apply these values to configuration[y/n]:y Applied values to configuration
"share Encrypt": "no"   Enabling encryption for every smb packet request and response will increase security but cost performance.

"case Sensitive": "no"   Setting this param to  no  will improve performance compared to setting this to  yes  "ms Dfs Support": "no"   Setting this param to  no  will improve performance compared to setting this to  yes  "enable User Based Enumeration": "no"   Setting this param to  no  will improve performance compared to setting this to  yes  "use Direct Io": "yes"   If the usecase has a lot of Direct IO then setting this to  yes  will improve the performance.

To setup and configure the Mo SMB server to work in the noauth mode for any Server Operating System, perform the following basic steps: Ensure that you have mentioned the correct storage path in /etc/mosmb/mosmb.json in Share Settings . In the /etc/mosmb/mosmb.json file, under section auth Section , set the "auth Mode": "no Auth" Start / Restart the Mo SMB server for the desired changes to take effect. In the noauth authentication mode, the only applicable authentication strategy (ACL) is none .

All Mo SMB-CL builds supports single domain clustering. To form a single domain Mo SMB cluster simply follow the configurations mentioned in steps below one by one. 1. One EDF node should have the clustershell configured for executing commands in parallel on a cluster. 2. Carry out Network Time Protocol (NTP) Configuration settings on all Mo SMB server nodes. 3. Install Kerberos packages on all Mo SMB server nodes

1. Request the Domain Administration team for AD and DNS Configurations. Create a Special user in the Active Directory (AD) (Step required to be carried out only once per Mo SMB cluster) Create Mo SMB Cluster Keytab (Step required to be carried out only once per Mo SMB cluster) Copy generated keytab file to all the Mo SMB server nodes which are needed in a cluster as mosmb.keytab at location /etc/mosmb/
Copy the mosmb.keytab in /etc/mosmb folder of one node Copy the keytab to all mosmb nodes clush -B -ac /etc/mosmb/mosmb.keytab
Carry out name resolution settings on all Mo SMB servers nodes. 2. Kerberos and related services configurations on all Mo SMB server nodes in the cluster.
1. On one node where clush is set, update the mosmb.json 1. Add the Product Key in the /etc/mosmb/mosmb.json file 2. mosmb.json parameters for cluster setup

* The mosmb housekeeping volume created with correct replication factor and low latency to be used as pstore * You can also use bindInterface parameter to bind a particular network interface to mosmb for the SMB traffic * In globalSection set enableHighAvailability to yes * In vinodeSection check that the "mechanism" is set to "zookeeper" * In persistentStoreSection keep pstorePath location on the common storage path on all MoSMB nodes in the cluster. * In section nodeInfoSection, assign a different nodeId to each node in MoSMB cluster. ( NOTE: this is a parameter that will differ on each of the mosmb nodes. ) * In section authSection, set authMode as ntlmLocal . ( NOTE: This will ensure krb5 authentication is tried first, if that is not successful, the new releases support ntlmSSP authentication, if that fails then ntlmLocal authentication is tried. ) * In shareSection keep sharePath location on the common storage path on all MoSMB nodes in the cluster. * In shareSection set parameters isContinouslyAvailable and isCluster to yes * In shareSection set parameter fsInfoAutoDetect to yes * In shareSection set parameter checkPosixPermissionBit to no * In shareSection set parameter useDirectIo to yes * In shareSection set parameters fileCreationMask and directoryCreationMask as per requirement For detailed mosmb.json parameters for cluster setup refer: step 8 in Setup the MoSMB configuration file for cluster use

3. Add all required shares to the mosmb.json. For this you can use the mosmbcli utility. First use the MCS or CLI or REST API to create volumes on the EDF cluster. Use this Path to add new share in mosmb.json

Example:  mosmbcli set --json shares -share_name <share name> -path <share path on EDF cluster> -is_cluster yes -fs_info_auto_detect yes -check_posix_permission_bit no -use_direct_io yes -file_creation_mask 0664 -directory_creation_mask 0775

3. Set the zookeeper server list in vinode section under host Port parameter in rotation. (keep the local node IP first in the list for better performance if zookeeper coexists with mosmb or else distribute the load by rotating the IPs)

clush -w node1name mosmbcli set --json node_info -node_id 1 clush -w node2name mosmbcli set --json node_info -node_id 2 clush -w node3name mosmbcli set --json node_info -node_id 3 clush -w node4name mosmbcli set --json node_info -node_id 4 clush -w node5name mosmbcli set --json node_info -node_id 5
* For machine: ulimit -a * For MoSMB service: ps aux | grep mosmb cat /proc/<pid>/limits
fuse.flush.inline value is set to 0 fuse.disable.writeback value is set to 1 fuse.use.compressed.inode.format value is set to 1
If you have a AD farm, and want mosmb to use only few of them for any reason create a list of AD IPs: Add Priority List for AD IPs in /etc/mosmb

A keytab is a file containing pairs of Kerberos principals and encrypted keys that are derived from the Kerberos password. One can use this file to log on to Kerberos without being prompted for a password.

Note : skip this step for Release versions:3.1.1_004 and above. After Domain join process is carried out /etc/krb5.keytab is created. Run ktutil commands to generate mosmb.keytab at location /etc/mosmb Command : ktutil ktutil: rkt /etc/krb5.keytab ktutil: l ktutil: delent <slot no for restricted entries> ktutil: wkt /etc/mosmb/mosmb.keytab ktutil: q
2. Add host keys with encryption to /etc/mosmb/mosmb.keytab

Run ktutil commands as follows: Command : ktutil ktutil: addent -password -p <domain user>@<DOMAIN NAME> -k 1 -e rc4-hmac Password for <domain user>@<domain name>: ktutil: addent -password -p <domain user>@<DOMAIN NAME> -k 1 -e aes256-cts Password for <domain user>@<domain name>: ktutil: wkt /etc/mosmb/mosmb.keytab ktutil: q

ktutil ktutil: addent -password -p mouser@MOSMBAD.DOM -k 1 -e rc4-hmac Password for mouser@MOSMBAD.DOM: ktutil: addent -password -p mouser@MOSMBAD.DOM -k 1 -e aes256-cts Password for mouser@MOSMBAD.DOM: ktutil: wkt /etc/mosmb/mosmb.keytab ktutil: q

1. The domain user used in above commands and the user used to generate the ticket with kinit should be the same. 2. The domain name in above commands must be in uppercase. 3. If and when the user password or user spn is changed Kerberos keytab related settings are needed to carry out again

The krb5 configuration file contains Kerberos configuration information, including the locations of KDCs and admin servers for the Kerberos realms of interest, defaults for the current realm and for Kerberos applications, and mappings of hostnames onto Kerberos realms. Warning: Please ensure correct name settings for KDC, AD, DNS host FQDNs from your network administrator.
Make sure these entries are present in the /etc/krb5.conf file.
includedir /etc/krb5.conf.d/ [libdefaults] .......... rdns = false default_ccache_name = FILE:/opt/mosmb/kerberos_tkt default_realm = MOSMBAD.DOM #Please note this is example. ..........
[realms] MOSMBAD.DOM = { kdc = mo-ad.mosmbad.dom master_kdc = mo-ad.mosmbad.dom } [domain_realm] mosmbad.dom = MOSMBAD.DOM .mosmbad.dom = MOSMBAD.DOM

# cat /etc/krb5.conf | grep default_ccache_name default_ccache_name = FILE:/opt/mosmb/mosmb_kerberos_ticket OR default_ccache_name = DIR:/opt/mosmb/kerberos_ticket_dir #default_ccache_name = KEYRING:persistent:%{uid} [not supported] # cat /etc/krb5.conf.d/kcm_default_ccache | grep default_ccache_name default_ccache_name = KCM:

1. MoSMB will support only cache type FILE for kerberos versions below 1.13 2. The FILE and DIR path can be of your choice. Make sure path is anything other than /tmp for persistence of cache across system reboots.

You must have a working model of the AD in your Evaluation and/or Production environment before you start using Mo SMB with Kerberos authentication. You require administrative rights of the AD machine to make related modifications.

In order to use Mo SMB server we need a special user in the AD Login to AD machine with Domain Administrator user. Enter the name of user for example - mo-special-user Enter the user logon name - UPN for example - mospecialuser@mosmbad.dom   Next Select This account supports Kerberos AES 128 bit encryption Select This account supports Kerberos AES 256 bit encryption
Every single standalone MoSMB server and MoSMB Cluster will have its own special user. Special user should be used to generate MoSMB cluster keytab from AD domain.

msdfs link can be created by using either server IP or FQDN ln -s msdfs:\\<ip>\\<share Name> <link Name> OR ln -s msdfs:\\<fqdn>\\<share Name> <link Name> In following example we are creating a msdfs link on Node 3 pointing to Node 1's exported share named as share1.

Command1 : mkdir /opt/share1 Command2 : cd /opt/share1 Command3 : ln -s msdfs:\\10.10.0.10\\share1 linka Command4 : ls -lah linka Output : lrwxrwxrwx 1 root root 30 Aug 20 12:58 linka -> 'msdfs:\10.10.0.10\share1' Command5 : ln -s msdfs:\\server1.mosmbad.dom\\share1 linkb Command6 : ls -lah linkb Output : lrwxrwxrwx 1 root root 30 Aug 20 12:58 linkb -> 'msdfs:\server1.mosmbad.dom\share1'

msdfs link can be created by using either server IP or FQDN ln -s msdfs:\\<ip>\\<share Name>,\\<ip>\\<share Name> <link Name> OR ln -s msdfs:\\<fqdn>\\<share Name>,\\<fqdn>\\<share Name> <link Name> In following example we are creating a msdfs link on Node 3 pointing to Node 1 and Node2's exported shares named as share1. Consider that Node1 and Node2 are in cluster and pointing to same storage exported over share1.

Command1 : mkdir /opt/share2 Command2 : cd /opt/share2 Command3 : ln -s msdfs:\\10.10.0.10\\share1,\\10.10.0.20\\share1 multilinka Command4 : ls -lah multilinka Output : lrwxrwxrwx 1 root root 30 Aug 20 12:58 multilinka -> 'msdfs:\10.10.0.10\share1,\10.10.0.20\share1' Command5 : ln -s msdfs:\\server1.mosmbad.dom\\share1,\\server2.mosmbad.dom\\share1 multilinkb Command6 : ls -lah multilinkb Output : lrwxrwxrwx 1 root root 30 Aug 20 12:58 multilinkb -> 'msdfs:\server1.mosmbad.dom\share1,\server2.mosmbad.dom\share1'

To support msdfs links with mosmb, some configuration changes are needed in /etc/mosmb/mosmb.json only on the node where links are created. Set the parameters under Section "shares Section" as "ms Dfs Support": "yes", and "metadata Store": "xattr" for share which has links.

Command : cat /etc/mosmb/mosmb.json | grep -e shareName -e sharePath -e msDfsSupport -e metadata Output on Node1 : "shareName": "share1", "sharePath": "/mnt/share1", "msDfsSupport": "no", "metadataStore": "none", Output on Node2 : "shareName": "share1", "sharePath": "/mnt/share1", "msDfsSupport": "no", "metadataStore": "none", Output on Node3 : "shareName": "share1", "sharePath": "/opt/share1/", "msDfsSupport": "yes", "metadataStore": "xattr", "shareName": "share2", "sharePath": "/opt/share2/", "msDfsSupport": "yes", "metadataStore": "xattr", "shareName": "localshare", "sharePath": "/home/share1", "msDfsSupport": "no", "metadataStore": "none",

1. If the format used while creating links is incorrect, then DFS link access will fail. 2. msdfs links created only on files are supported currently. Links created on directory are not fully supported. 3. If msDfsSupport parameter is set to no in mosmb.json where links are crated, then the links even though they are present on server side, will not be enumerated on the client side. 4. Make sure permissions on all nodes for shares are for same user and group in case of domain users permissions. 5.1 Make sure permissions on all nodes for shares are for same user and group and the uid and gid are are same on all servers. 5.2 Make sure that local users are added as mosmb users and have same password on all servers. 6. If any of the permissions related changes are not carried out then link may not be accessible and throw the permissions related issues.
