Important : All the components - AD, Mo SMB Server, and SMB client machines should always be time synced for the AD-Kerberos authentication. Start or restart this Network Time Protocol (ntp) / (chronyd) service on the Mo SMB server.
Command : yum install ntp or apt-get install ntp Command : systemctl stop ntpd.service or service ntp stop Command : systemctl start ntpd.service or service ntp start

Note : To resolve the Fully Qualified Domain Name(FQDN) of a Network Time Protocol(NTP) server, ensure that your Domain Name Server(DNS) settings are well configured. Warning : The Kerberos authentication fails in case: The time between the Mo SMB server, AD and SMB clients is unsynced.

Mo SMB supports Microsoft Active Directory (AD) based Kerberos authentication. The Mo SMB server is added to the AD Domain to authenticate and authorize the SMB Clients's access to Mo SMB shares. Mo SMB relies on the internal transitive trusts created between different domains in the AD Forest to authenticate the authorized AD users from other Domains to access the Mo SMB shares.

A single Mo SMB server can join multiple AD Domains of different Forests to allow users of different AD Forests to access the Shares when configured as part of a Public Cloud Infrastructure. From the Mo SMB server run one of the following utility to carry out a domain join process:

Install likewise-open Command : sudo apt-get install likewise-open To join the domain: Command : domainjoin-cli join <AD_Domain_Name> <AD_Domain_Administrator> [<password>] Note :If the password is not supplied on command line, you will be prompted to specify it at the next prompt. To list the current domain: Command : domainjoin-cli query
Before a domain join verify the /etc/resolv.conf . It should ideally contain the domain related information.
Command : cat /etc/resolv.conf Output : # Generated by NetworkManager search ad.example.com nameserver xx.xx.x.xx nameserver xxx.xx.xxx.x nameserver x.x.x.x

Install the realm and dependent packages on the server machine. Command : yum install realmd OR apt-get install realm Search for the domain you wish to join. Command : realm discover <domain name> Note: <domain name> here is the search value from the /etc/resolv.conf file, that is ad.example.com. To join the domain with realm. Command : realm join --user= <username> <domain name> Note: The domain join creates a machine entry in the AD. You must update the DNS entry manually.

Before a domain join verify the /etc/resolv.conf file. It should ideally contain the domain related information. Refer to the example in the Realm Join steps. Install the realm and dependent packages on the server machine. Command : yum install adcli OR apt-get install adcli To join the domain with adcli: Command : adcli join --service-name=host -S <domain_name> -U <domain_administrator_user> --host-fqdn= <server_hostname>.<domain_name> Note: The domain join creates a machine entry in the AD.

You must update the DNS entry manually. To leave a domain with adcli: Command : adcli delete-computer --domain= <domain_name> -U <domain_administrator_user> Note: This deletes the entry from AD. You must update the DNS entries manually.

We do not recommend switching between the ACL strategies over existing data. For a share, switching between the ACL strategies over existing data will result in change of owner and group mapping for smb user equivalent Linux user. In NTACL scheme the file ownership is owner/group as root/root. Windows ACL are stored in metadata store for the file.

In Posix ACL scheme the smb session user is mapped as per current service mentioned in /etc/nsswitch.conf So in case /etc/nsswitch.conf has SSSD settings it will map to UID/GID based on settings in /etc/sssd.conf . If a user changes these schemes for existing shares from ntlm Local authentication to krb5 authentication , sssd configuration is needed for user id mapping. This will shift the user mapping scheme from local users to the one mapped by SSSD.

The local linux users will not be mapped to the domain users in such case for the shared files or folders. So we suggest that network administrator should not switch between authentication schemes and ACL schemes for existing shares. So that permissions are retained. In case the switching of scheme is must network administrator would have to do file owner/group changes to share manually to adapt to the new scheme of ACL and authentication.

sssd helps during the User ID mapping for domain users. If in case sssd is not installed before, install sssd-common and configure sssd on the Mo SMB server machine. Create a sssd configuration file for Mo SMB /etc/sssd/conf.d/mosmb_sssd.conf and it should contain parameters as shown below.

[sssd] domains = mosmbad.dom config_file_version = 2 services = nss, pam [domain/mosmbad.dom] ad_domain = mosmbad.dom krb5_realm = MOSMBAD.DOM krb5_keytab = /etc/mosmb/mosmb.keytab realmd_tags = manages-system joined-with-adcli cache_credentials = True id_provider = ad krb5_store_password_if_offline = True default_shell = /bin/bash ldap_id_mapping = True use_fully_qualified_names = True fallback_homedir = /home/%u@%d access_provider = ad
sssd configuration files must be at /etc/sssd/ with permissions of file set to 600 (-rw-------) and for both /etc/sssd/sssd.conf and /etc/sssd/conf.d/mosmb_sssd.conf

The krb5 configuration file contains Kerberos configuration information, including the locations of KDCs and admin servers for the Kerberos realms of interest, defaults for the current realm and for Kerberos applications, and mappings of hostnames onto Kerberos realms. Warning: Please ensure correct name settings for KDC, AD, DNS host FQDNs from your network administrator.
Make sure these entries are present in the /etc/krb5.conf file.
includedir /etc/krb5.conf.d/ [libdefaults] .......... rdns = false default_ccache_name = DIR:/opt/mosmb/kerberos_tkt default_realm = MOSMBAD.DOM ..........

Add the details of all domains a new file /etc/krb5.conf.d/mosmb_krb5.conf depending on your IT infrastructure requirements Add realms section with kdc and admin_server entries for each domain that mosmb node belongs to.

[realms] MOSMBAD.DOM = { kdc = mo-ad.mosmbad.dom master_kdc = mo-ad.mosmbad.dom } MOSMBAD2.DOM = { kdc = mo-ad2.mosmbad2.dom master_kdc = mo-ad2.mosmbad2.dom } [domain_realm] mosmbad.dom = MOSMBAD.DOM .mosmbad.dom = MOSMBAD.DOM mosmbad2.dom = MOSMBAD2.DOM .mosmbad2.dom = MOSMBAD2.DOM

Kerberos 1.13 and above, Mo SMB support cache types FILE, KCM, DIR. keyring is not supported. Though cache type FILE is supported for multidomain configurations use DIR or KCM cache so that multiple tickets are stored.

# cat /etc/krb5.conf | grep default_ccache_name default_ccache_name = DIR:/opt/mosmb/kerberos_ticket_dir #default_ccache_name = KEYRING:persistent:%{uid} [not supported] # cat /etc/krb5.conf.d/kcm_default_ccache | grep default_ccache_name default_ccache_name = KCM:

1. Mention one of the Domain in as default in krb5.conf file out of all domains to be configured. 2. MoSMB will support only cache type FILE for ' kerberos versions below 1.13 so ideally update Kerberos version on the servers where multi-domain configurations are needed. 3. The DIR path can be of your choice. Make sure path is anything other than /tmp for persistence of cache across system reboots.

In cases where we do not want the full cluster downtime, we may take up rolling upgrades with mosmb package upgrade on each node at a time. The smb service will never be down for the cluster. It will be down only for the nodes under upgrade. New connections will go to the nodes that have mosmb service up and running.

When the mosmb service on these nodes (where upgrade is completed) comes up, the whole cluster should be up and at this point the new connections can be served by all the nodes in the cluster again. We can now move to the upgrade of mosmb service for the next node. The active smb connections, if any from the nodes where mosmb was brought down, that were initiated as part of the mosmb cluster, will failover to other active nodes of the cluster.

Note : After stopping mosmb service, wait for 5 minutes to make sure all connections from this node failover to rest of the cluster nodes Note : When the mosmb service on this nodes is back up, those connections will not fail back to it. New connections can go to the new node as part of the cluster.
stop mosmb service from dashboard (on the two nodes selected for upgrade) Check service status of mosmb to make sure all mosmb processes have stopped

A keytab is a file containing pairs of Kerberos principals and encrypted keys that are derived from the Kerberos password. One can use this file to log on to Kerberos without being prompted for a password. 1. Add user keys of all domains with encryption to /etc/mosmb/mosmb.keytab

Run ktutil commands as follows: Command : ktutil ktutil: addent -password -p <domain user>@<DOMAIN NAME> -k 1 -e rc4-hmac Password for <domain user>@<domain name>: ktutil: addent -password -p <domain user>@<DOMAIN NAME> -k 1 -e aes256-cts Password for <domain user>@<domain name>: ktutil: wkt /etc/mosmb/mosmb.keytab ktutil: q Example : ktutil ktutil: addent -password -p mouser@MOSMBAD.DOM -k 1 -e rc4-hmac Password for mouser1@MOSMBAD.DOM: ktutil: addent -password -p mouser@MOSMBAD.DOM -k 1 -e aes256-cts Password for mouser1@MOSMBAD.DOM: ktutil: wkt /etc/mosmb/mosmb.keytab ktutil: q

Run klist command as follows: Command : klist -k /etc/mosmb/mosmb.keytab Output : should be similar to - Keytab name: FILE:/etc/mosmb/mosmb.keytab KVNO Principal ----- -------------------------------------------------------------------------- 1 mouser1@MOSMBAD.DOM 1 mouser1@MOSMBAD.DOM 1 mouser2@MOSMBAD2.DOM 1 mouser2@MOSMBAD2.DOM

1. Depending on the encryption types supported by the AD domain, entries in the keytab file will increase. (choose the encryption type as per your IT infrastructure requirements) 2. The domain user used in above commands and the user used to generate the ticket with kinit should be the same. (should be same as the one mentioned in /etc/mosmb/domain_list.txt as mentioned in next steps) 3. The domain name in above commands must be in uppercase . 4. If and when the user password or user spn is changed Kerberos keytab related settings are needed to be carried out again

The Domain Name Server (DNS) configuration is an important activity during the authentication. The authentication between SMB Clients and the Mo SMB server will not work as expected if DNS configurations are not carried out.

You must have a working model of the DNS in your Evaluation and/or Production environment before you start using the appliance. You require Administrative Rights of the DNS machine to make related modifications. To edit the DNS server settings manually, please coordinate with your DNS Manager or Network Administrator . Validate if the entry for the Mo SMB server is added to the corresponding DNS server(s).

Delete all the entries containing hostname to ip address mapping of the cluster nodes in both - the forward and reverse lookup zones if any present. Assign a common hostname (Mo SMB cluster name) to all the nodes in the cluster. For example - my-mosmb-cluster Under Forward Lookup Zones , select the desired domain name. for example - mosmbad.dom Add new entry: Menu   Action   New Host (A or AAAA)...

for all the nodes in the cluster Fill in the related information : Name is the Mo SMB cluster name for example - my-mosmb-cluster IP address is the IP address of the Mo SMB server. for example - 10.20.0.11 Add the Host. Repeat this procedure to add other nodes of cluster against same Mo SMB cluster name. Add new entry: Menu   Action   New Host (A or AAAA)...

for all the nodes in the cluster Fill in the related information : Name is the Mo SMB cluster name for example - my-mosmb-cluster IP address is the IP address of the Mo SMB server. for example - 10.20.0.11 Name is the Mo SMB cluster name for example - my-mosmb-cluster IP address is the IP address of the Mo SMB server. for example - 10.20.0.11 Repeat this procedure to add other nodes of cluster against same Mo SMB cluster name.
